status.complexityHint: this may take a few minutes depending on password complexity
status.crackedDescription: your pdf has been unlocked and is ready to download
status.failedDescription: the password was too strong for our current wordlist.
status.failedHint: try a different file or a weaker password.

process.heading: the process
steps: You upload a password-protected PDF (max 10 MB). We hash the file with SHA-256 — duplicate uploads reuse the same job.
pdf2john extracts the encryption hash embedded inside the PDF.
hashcat runs a brute-force attack using numeric masks (?d), trying every combination from 1 to 8 digits in sequence.
Once the password is found, qpdf decrypts the file. The unlocked PDF is ready for download.

limitations.heading: limitations
items: Only all-numeric passwords are supported. Alphabetic, mixed, or special-character passwords will not be cracked.
Max password length is 8 digits. An 8-digit password has 100 million combinations and may take several minutes.
Processing time grows exponentially with length — a 6-digit password cracks in seconds, an 8-digit one may take minutes.
File status is kept in memory and lost on server restart.
Files are not deleted automatically — do not upload sensitive documents.

manifesto.title: // manifesto.txt
manifesto.subtitle: why we built this
paragraphs: PDFs with passwords are a friction tax on your own documents.
The average password-protected PDF has a 4-digit numeric password. Set years ago. By someone who no longer works there.
You didn't lose the content — someone just put a padlock on something that was already yours.
We built this because we were tired of emailing 'hey, what's the password?' and waiting three days for a reply that never came.
No proprietary software. No sign-up. No cloud storage. Drop it, crack it, download it.
Passwords protect secrets. Your quarterly report is not a secret.
manifesto.signature: — the team that hates pdf passwords

contact.title: get in touch.
contact.subtitle: drop a message — we'll get back to you.

success.title: message sent.
